Questions: Why does Alexander Elder argue that timing a trade requires multiple timeframes?
Answers: Elder argues that a single timeframe gives an incomplete and potentially misleading picture of the market. Different timeframes often produce conflicting signals from the same indicator — a trend may be up on the weekly chart and down on the daily. Using multiple timeframes lets the trader understand the market's full context and find trades where the short-term and long-term signals agree.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What fundamental problem with technical indicators did Elder identify that led him to develop the Triple Screen?
Answers: Elder observed that trend-following indicators (such as moving averages and MACD) work well in trending markets but give false signals in sideways ranges, while oscillators (such as Stochastic and RSI) work well in ranges but produce premature signals during strong trends. No single indicator could reliably handle all market conditions, so Elder designed a multi-screen system that combines the strengths of both categories.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Alexander Elder Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/alexander-elder-triple-screen-strategy/

Questions: What was Elder's original source of inspiration for the tide-wave-ripple framework?
Answers: The tide, wave, and ripple framework was originally articulated by Robert Rhea, one of the first technical analysts, who labelled long-term trends as tides, intermediate-term movements as waves, and short-term fluctuations as ripples. Elder adopted this framework and formalised it into the Triple Screen system, refining the timeframes to suit modern markets.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is the core trading principle behind the Triple Screen's approach to timing?
Answers: The core principle is to trade only in the direction of the market's dominant tide (long-term trend), enter on intermediate-term waves (pullbacks or rallies against the tide), and pinpoint exact entries using short-term ripples (intraday breakouts). This three-layer approach ensures trades are taken with the momentum of the larger trend rather than against it.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder say that trading against the dominant trend is the most dangerous mistake?
Answers: Trading against the dominant tide means fighting a force far larger than any individual trader. Even if short-term signals favour a counter-trend position, the dominant trend will reassert itself and overwhelm those positions. Elder teaches that the odds of success are dramatically higher when every trade is aligned with the market's primary direction.
Label: Timing
Source: MQL5 – Elder's Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.mql5.com/en/blogs/post/731016

Questions: When was the Triple Screen Trading System first published, and where?
Answers: The Triple Screen Trading System was first published in 1986 in Futures Magazine, making it one of the earliest formalized multi-timeframe trading frameworks in technical analysis literature.
Label: Timing
Source: AskTraders – Elder's Triple Screening Strategy | https://www.asktraders.com/learn-to-trade/trading-guide/elders-triple-screening-strategy/

Questions: What does Elder mean when he says timing is the 'hardest part' of trading?
Answers: Elder teaches that identifying the correct direction of a trade is relatively straightforward — a good system correctly identifies the trend most of the time. The hard part is deciding precisely when to enter and when to exit: too early and the trade is stopped out by normal noise; too late and much of the potential profit has already been made. The entire Triple Screen is designed to solve this timing problem.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the factor of five relate to timeframe selection in Elder's system?
Answers: Elder recommends selecting timeframes that differ from each other by approximately a factor of five. For example, if the intermediate trading timeframe is the daily chart, the long-term chart should be the weekly (approximately five trading days = one week). If the intermediate is the 4-hour chart, the long-term should be the daily (approximately five 4-hour bars = one trading day). This ratio ensures the timeframes are genuinely different in character rather than superficially similar.
Label: Timing
Source: TradingStrategyGuides – The Triple Screen | https://tradingstrategyguides.com/alexander-elder-trading-strategy-the-triple-screen/

Questions: What does Elder say about traders who use only one timeframe?
Answers: Elder compares trading with a single timeframe to driving with one eye closed — technically possible but unnecessarily dangerous. A trader using only one timeframe misses crucial context that is immediately visible on a higher or lower timeframe. They cannot distinguish between a strong trend and a corrective rally, or between a genuine breakout and noise.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the factor of 4–6 multiplier in Elder's timeframe selection?
Answers: Elder's factor of 4–6 is used to divide charts into meaningful timeframe levels. When moving from the trading (intermediate) timeframe to the entry (short-term) timeframe, the trader divides by a factor of 4 to 6. For example, from a daily chart (intermediate), dividing by 6 gives approximately a 4-hour chart for the entry screen. This factor ensures there is enough difference between screens to filter out noise.
Label: Timing
Source: TradingStrategyGuides – The Triple Screen | https://tradingstrategyguides.com/alexander-elder-trading-strategy-the-triple-screen/

Questions: What does Elder say about the relationship between weekly planning and daily execution?
Answers: Elder strongly recommends completing the analytical work for the first two screens — establishing the weekly tide and identifying the daily oscillator conditions — on the weekend before markets open. The third screen (execution) is then handled during market hours. This separation between planning and execution reduces the influence of emotions during the trading day.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: Why does Elder prefer the weekly chart to anchor his timing analysis?
Answers: The weekly chart filters out daily noise and reflects the considered opinions of professional traders who drive market direction over multiple sessions. Elder believes that the weekly chart represents the dominant tide more reliably than any daily or intraday chart, and that anchoring the entire timing framework to this timeframe significantly improves signal quality.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the difficulty of timing at the extreme right edge of a chart?
Answers: Elder acknowledges that trends and ranges are easy to identify in the middle of a chart — in hindsight — but the closer a trader gets to the right edge (the present moment), the foggier the picture becomes. This is why combining multiple indicators and screens is essential: no single view at the right edge is reliable enough on its own.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder's timing approach reflect his views on market efficiency?
Answers: Elder does not believe markets are perfectly efficient. He believes that prices oscillate around fair value — defined by the exponential moving average — and that these oscillations are predictable enough to time trades profitably if the trader analyses multiple timeframes and uses a disciplined, rule-based approach. The market's predictability comes not from secret information but from the repetitive psychological patterns of crowds.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder say that a trader cannot time markets successfully without understanding crowd psychology?
Answers: Elder teaches that every price chart is a recording of the collective emotions — fear, greed, hope, and panic — of thousands of market participants. Timing a trade successfully means reading where crowd sentiment is in its cycle: extreme optimism signals an approaching reversal; extreme pessimism signals a potential buying opportunity. The oscillators in the Triple Screen measure this crowd psychology in real time.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about 'managing versus forecasting' in market timing?
Answers: Elder explicitly states that he does not try to forecast the market — he manages his response to what the market is doing. The distinction is crucial for timing: a forecaster sets a price target and waits for it; a manager responds to what the chart is showing at each screen and adjusts the timing of entry and exit dynamically based on current evidence.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder's concept of price oscillating around 'value' drive his timing?
Answers: Elder teaches that price and value are different things — price can be far above or below its fair value (represented by the exponential moving average). The most favourable timing for long entries is when price has pulled back to value — near the EMA in an uptrend — rather than when it is extended far above it. This 'buy-at-value' timing principle underpins all of Elder's entry decisions.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What makes the Triple Screen different from simply using three timeframes simultaneously?
Answers: The Triple Screen is not simply three charts side by side. Each screen has a specific and non-overlapping purpose: the first screen identifies the dominant trend and sets the allowed trading direction; the second identifies the optimal entry timing window; and the third pinpoints the precise entry price. The screens are hierarchically linked — only signals from the first screen determine which second-screen signals are valid.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Alexander Elder Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/alexander-elder-triple-screen-strategy/

Questions: How does Elder describe the 'stereo vision' benefit of multi-timeframe analysis?
Answers: Elder uses the metaphor of stereo vision — two eyes producing depth perception that a single eye cannot provide. A single-timeframe trader has a flat, one-dimensional view of the market. A multi-timeframe trader sees market structure in depth: the weekly chart provides context; the daily chart provides timing opportunity; the intraday chart provides precise execution. Together they create a three-dimensional picture.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the timing implications of strong uptrends for oscillator use?
Answers: In strong uptrends, Elder warns that oscillators will frequently reach overbought levels and stay there — generating premature sell signals that would cause the trader to exit winning positions far too early. The first screen's job is to identify these strong trends and instruct the trader to ignore all oscillator sell signals in that direction, acting only on the oversold (pullback) readings.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is the First Screen in Elder's Triple Screen System?
Answers: The First Screen is the long-term chart — typically a weekly chart when the trader works with daily charts. It uses trend-following indicators to identify the dominant market 'tide': the overall direction in which the market is moving. The First Screen establishes whether the trader is allowed to go long, go short, or stand aside.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What indicator does Elder originally recommend for the First Screen?
Answers: In the original Triple Screen design, Elder recommends applying the MACD histogram to the weekly chart. The slope of the weekly MACD histogram — whether it is rising or falling — determines the direction of the dominant tide. A rising histogram indicates a bullish tide; a falling histogram indicates a bearish tide.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Can traders use indicators other than MACD for the First Screen?
Answers: Yes. Elder acknowledges that any trend-following indicator can serve on the First Screen — including a simple moving average, a trend line, or the Directional Movement System (ADX). The key requirement is that it clearly identifies the market's dominant direction over a timeframe at least five times longer than the trader's intended trading timeframe.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Alexander Elder Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/alexander-elder-triple-screen-strategy/

Questions: How does the rising MACD histogram affect entry direction on the First Screen?
Answers: When the weekly MACD histogram is rising — even if it is still below the zero line — Elder treats this as a bullish tide. In this condition, the trader is permitted to look only for long entries on the Second Screen. All sell signals from the Second Screen are ignored until the weekly histogram begins to fall.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the falling MACD histogram affect entry direction on the First Screen?
Answers: When the weekly MACD histogram is falling — even if it is still above the zero line — Elder treats this as a bearish tide. The trader is permitted to look only for short-sell entries on the Second Screen. All buy signals from the Second Screen are ignored until the weekly histogram begins to rise.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does it mean when the weekly MACD histogram is rising but still in negative territory?
Answers: A rising MACD histogram that is still below zero indicates that while the market has not yet fully recovered from a bearish episode, bearish momentum is fading and bullish momentum is returning. Elder treats this as a bullish tide because the direction of the histogram's slope is more important than its position relative to zero.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does it mean when the weekly MACD histogram is falling but still in positive territory?
Answers: A falling MACD histogram that is still above zero indicates that bullish momentum is fading even though the market has not yet turned bearish. Elder treats this as a bearish tide because the slope of the histogram is the primary timing signal — the falling slope shows that the bulls are losing power.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say should happen if the First Screen produces an unclear signal?
Answers: If the First Screen is unclear — the weekly chart shows a mixed, choppy, or sideways market with no definable trend — Elder's advice is direct: do not proceed to the Second Screen. Move on to a different security where the tide direction is clear. Trading without a defined tide means accepting unnecessarily low-probability entries.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Alexander Elder Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/alexander-elder-triple-screen-strategy/

Questions: How does Elder use moving averages on the First Screen?
Answers: When using moving averages on the First Screen, Elder recommends applying a pair of exponential moving averages — a fast EMA and a slow EMA — to the long-term chart. When both EMAs are rising and the faster EMA is above the slower one, the tide is bullish. When both are falling and the faster is below the slower, the tide is bearish. The zone between them defines the 'value zone.'
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'value zone' and how does it relate to the First Screen?
Answers: The value zone is the area between the fast and slow exponential moving averages on any chart. Elder teaches that price oscillates above and below this zone: when price is deep in or below the value zone, it represents an attractive entry opportunity in an uptrend; when far above the value zone, it is extended and not a good entry. The First Screen identifies that the trend is bullish so that entries in the value zone on the Second Screen carry the highest probability.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does a trader use the Dow Theory concept of tides to calibrate the First Screen?
Answers: Robert Rhea's application of Dow Theory defines tides as the longest, most powerful market movements — lasting months to years. Elder maps this concept to the weekly chart in the Triple Screen, where the weekly MACD or EMA slope identifies this dominant force. The First Screen tells the trader whether the current moment sits inside a long-term bull tide, bear tide, or transitional period.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is the significance of the weekly chart for swing traders?
Answers: For a swing trader whose primary trading timeframe is the daily chart, the weekly chart is one order of magnitude larger — providing context that the daily chart cannot show. The weekly chart reveals whether a daily pullback is a buying opportunity within a larger uptrend or the beginning of a genuine reversal. Without checking the weekly First Screen, a daily pullback looks identical in both cases.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the First Screen protect traders from the most common timing error?
Answers: The most common timing error Elder identifies is entering a trade on a short-term signal that is actually moving against the dominant trend. The First Screen prevents this entirely by defining the only permitted trading direction before any other analysis begins. If the tide is up, no short signals are evaluated; if the tide is down, no buy signals are evaluated.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the First Screen's role for day traders using intraday charts?
Answers: A day trader whose intermediate timeframe is the 1-hour chart uses a 4- or 6-hour chart (or the daily chart) as the First Screen. The same principle applies: the First Screen on the longer intraday timeframe identifies the dominant intraday tide, and only trades in that direction are taken on the 1-hour Second Screen.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does a new high on the weekly chart affect timing decisions?
Answers: A weekly chart making new highs — with the MACD histogram rising and both EMAs pointing upward — represents a bullish tide at maximum strength. Elder treats this as a high-conviction environment for long entries on the Second Screen: any pullback to the value zone on the daily chart becomes an ideal timing opportunity.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about changing the direction of trade when the weekly tide reverses?
Answers: When the weekly MACD histogram changes direction — shifting from rising to falling — the tide has reversed and the trader must immediately switch the direction of all new trades. Existing long positions should be exited at the next favourable opportunity; the trader is now looking only for short setups on the Second Screen.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is a 'new high' divergence on the First Screen and why is it significant for timing?
Answers: A new high divergence on the weekly chart occurs when price makes a new all-time or multi-week high but the weekly MACD histogram fails to confirm with its own new high, making a lower peak instead. This bearish divergence on the First Screen is Elder's most powerful warning that the bullish tide is weakening and that the timing of new long entries should be paused.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is a 'new low' divergence on the First Screen?
Answers: A new low divergence occurs when price makes a new multi-week low on the weekly chart but the MACD histogram makes a shallower low than at the previous trough — indicating that bearish momentum is fading. Elder treats this bullish divergence as a powerful signal that the tide may be about to reverse upward, and shifts focus to long setups on the Second Screen.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How long should a trader wait on the First Screen before the tide is clear?
Answers: Elder does not prescribe a fixed waiting period. The tide is clear when the weekly MACD histogram has a definable upward or downward slope across at least two consecutive bars, confirming direction. A single-bar change is insufficient — the trader waits for two bars moving in the same direction before treating the new slope as the confirmed tide direction.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder apply the First Screen to markets other than stocks — such as forex and futures?
Answers: The First Screen principle is universal across all liquid markets. For forex, the weekly chart of the currency pair provides the tide; for futures, the weekly chart of the commodity or financial future is used. The only adjustment is ensuring the chosen trend indicator's parameters are appropriate for the volatility characteristics of the specific market.
Label: Timing
Source: AskTraders – Elder's Triple Screening Strategy | https://www.asktraders.com/learn-to-trade/trading-guide/elders-triple-screening-strategy/

Questions: What is the Second Screen in Elder's Triple Screen System?
Answers: The Second Screen is the intermediate-term chart — typically the daily chart when the First Screen uses the weekly. It uses an oscillator rather than a trend-following indicator to identify the 'wave': a short-term price movement that runs counter to the dominant tide. The wave creates the optimal timing window by giving the trader a discounted entry within the established trend.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does the Second Screen use an oscillator instead of a trend indicator?
Answers: The Second Screen uses an oscillator because the trader is no longer trying to identify the trend direction — that was established on the First Screen. Instead, the goal is to identify pullbacks and counter-trend moves within the tide: temporary dips in an uptrend or temporary rallies in a downtrend. Oscillators are the ideal tools for this because they measure overbought and oversold conditions that signal the wave's exhaustion point.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Alexander Elder Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/alexander-elder-triple-screen-strategy/

Questions: What oscillators does Elder recommend for the Second Screen?
Answers: Elder recommends four primary oscillators for the Second Screen: his own Force Index (using a 2-day EMA), Elder-Ray (Bull Power and Bear Power), Stochastic, and Williams %R. All four measure the oscillation of price momentum, but with different mathematical approaches. Elder acknowledges that any quality oscillator the trader is comfortable with will work.
Label: Timing
Source: BrokerXplorer – Master the Triple Screen | https://www.brokerxplorer.com/article/how-to-master-the-triple-screen-trading-strategy-1547

Questions: What is the Second Screen's entry condition when the tide is bullish?
Answers: When the weekly tide is bullish (rising MACD histogram), Elder watches the daily oscillator on the Second Screen for a decline into oversold territory. An oversold reading in the direction of the tide signals that the counter-trend wave (the daily pullback) is exhausted and that the tide's upward force is likely to reassert. This oversold moment is the optimal timing window for a long entry.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is the Second Screen's entry condition when the tide is bearish?
Answers: When the weekly tide is bearish (falling MACD histogram), Elder watches the daily oscillator on the Second Screen for a rally into overbought territory. An overbought reading signals that the counter-trend wave (the daily bounce in a downtrend) is exhausted and that the tide's downward force is about to resume. This overbought moment is the optimal timing window for a short entry.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: Why does Elder focus on finding pullbacks rather than chasing breakouts?
Answers: Elder's timing philosophy is built on buying at value — entering when price has temporarily moved against the trend and is therefore cheaper and lower risk than at breakout prices. Chasing a breakout means entering at the extreme of the move, far from the stop-loss, with an unfavourable risk-reward ratio. Pullbacks to the value zone provide better entry prices and allow tighter stops.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the Force Index work as a Second Screen oscillator?
Answers: The Force Index is Elder's own indicator that multiplies the day's price change by volume. A two-day EMA of the Force Index is applied on the daily chart as the Second Screen oscillator. In a weekly uptrend, a negative Force Index reading (short-term selling driving price below the prior day) signals that the daily wave has created a buying opportunity. In a weekly downtrend, a positive Force Index reading signals a counter-trend rally has created a shorting opportunity.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does Elder-Ray work as a Second Screen oscillator?
Answers: Elder-Ray consists of two components: Bull Power (the daily high minus the 13-day EMA) and Bear Power (the daily low minus the 13-day EMA). In a weekly uptrend, Elder looks for Bear Power to turn negative but begin rising back toward zero — signalling that bears are losing control during the daily wave and a long entry is approaching. In a downtrend, he looks for Bull Power to turn positive but begin falling — signalling that bulls are losing control and a short entry is near.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does Stochastic work as a Second Screen oscillator?
Answers: Stochastic compares the closing price to the range of recent prices, oscillating between 0 and 100. In a weekly uptrend, Elder waits for the daily Stochastic to fall below 20 (oversold) and then turn back upward — the crossover of the K line above the D line from below 20 is the timing signal for a long entry. In a downtrend, a Stochastic rise above 80 (overbought) followed by a K-line crossover below the D-line from above 80 signals a short entry.
Label: Timing
Source: BrokerXplorer – Master the Triple Screen | https://www.brokerxplorer.com/article/how-to-master-the-triple-screen-trading-strategy-1547

Questions: How does Williams %R work as a Second Screen oscillator?
Answers: Williams %R oscillates between 0 and -100. Readings above -20 indicate overbought conditions; readings below -80 indicate oversold conditions. In a weekly uptrend, Elder watches for the daily Williams %R to fall below -80 and then begin rising back toward -50 — this upturn from oversold territory in the direction of the bullish tide is the timing signal for a long entry.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What does Elder say about Williams %R divergences on the Second Screen?
Answers: Elder considers divergences between Williams %R and price on the daily chart to be among the highest-quality timing signals available. A bullish divergence — where price makes a new low but %R makes a shallower low — indicates that the downward wave is losing momentum while the bullish tide remains intact. This combination of divergence and oversold condition represents an exceptionally well-timed entry.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is the Stochastic K-line and D-line crossover and why does Elder use it?
Answers: The Stochastic oscillator has two lines: K (faster) and D (slower, a moving average of K). When K crosses above D from below 20 (oversold) in a weekly uptrend, this crossover confirms that the daily wave has turned upward in alignment with the tide — providing both an oscillator signal and confirmation via the crossover pattern. When K crosses below D from above 80 (overbought) in a downtrend, it signals a short entry.
Label: Timing
Source: DailyForex – Elder Triple Screen System | https://www.dailyforex.com/forex-articles/2020/09/elder-triple-screen-system/151378

Questions: Why does Elder warn against selling short when Stochastic is oversold?
Answers: If the weekly tide is bullish and the daily Stochastic is oversold, selling short is trading directly against two layers of evidence: the tide and the oscillator are both saying the next major move is up. Elder's rule is categorical: when Stochastic is oversold, do not sell short. When it is overbought, do not buy. Only take signals in the direction of the tide.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What does Elder say about the Stochastic K and D lines moving in the same direction?
Answers: When both the K line and the D line are moving in the same direction, Elder treats this as confirmation that the short-term trend is well-established. If both are rising during an oversold recovery in a weekly uptrend, the bullish signal is stronger. If both are falling in an overbought reversal during a weekly downtrend, the bearish signal is reinforced.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does Elder prevent the Second Screen from generating premature signals in a strong trend?
Answers: In a strong, persistent uptrend, the daily oscillator may rarely reach oversold levels — it may hover in mid-range or stay near overbought. Elder's solution is to wait for any meaningful pullback that causes the oscillator to decline, even if it does not reach the extreme oversold level. The directional slope of the oscillator — moving in the direction of the tide — is sufficient to indicate that the wave has provided an entry opportunity.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the RSI function as a Second Screen oscillator for Elder?
Answers: The RSI (Relative Strength Index) oscillates between 0 and 100, with readings above 70 considered overbought and below 30 oversold. While not Elder's original choice, he acknowledges RSI as a valid Second Screen oscillator. In a weekly uptrend, a daily RSI falling below 30 and turning back upward signals the timing opportunity for a long entry.
Label: Timing
Source: RoboForex Blog – Triple Screen Trading System | https://blog.roboforex.com/blog/2020/02/18/triple-screen-trading-system-how-to/

Questions: What does Elder say about waiting for the oscillator to leave the overbought/oversold zone before entry?
Answers: Elder does not always require the oscillator to fully exit the extreme zone before acting. For the Force Index and Elder-Ray, a simple reversal of slope from the extreme is sufficient. For Stochastic and Williams %R, Elder prefers to see the oscillator beginning to move back toward the midpoint — demonstrating that the wave's exhaustion point has been passed and the tide is reasserting control.
Label: Timing
Source: DailyForex – Elder Triple Screen System | https://www.dailyforex.com/forex-articles/2020/09/elder-triple-screen-system/151378

Questions: What is the MACD histogram's role specifically on the Second Screen?
Answers: On the Second Screen (daily chart), the MACD histogram's slope — whether it is rising or falling on a bar-by-bar basis — provides the wave timing signal. In a weekly uptrend, Elder looks for the daily MACD histogram to turn from falling to rising (even if still below zero) as confirmation that the daily wave has bottomed and the trend is resuming. This slope change is the day-by-day trigger for the entry zone.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: What is a divergence between the Second Screen oscillator and price?
Answers: A divergence occurs when price moves to a new extreme but the oscillator fails to confirm with its own new extreme. In a weekly uptrend, if the daily chart's price makes a new short-term low but the oscillator makes a higher low than the previous trough, this bullish divergence — combined with the bullish tide from the First Screen — represents an exceptionally strong timing signal for a long entry.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does Elder use the two-day EMA of the Force Index specifically on the Second Screen?
Answers: The two-day EMA of the Force Index is Elder's preferred oscillator for the Second Screen because it is sensitive enough to catch short-term waves without being so noisy that it generates constant false signals. In a weekly uptrend, Elder adds to long positions whenever the 2-day Force Index EMA turns negative — signalling that short-term selling pressure has created a momentary dip while the larger tide remains bullish.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is Bear Power in the Elder-Ray indicator and how does it time long entries?
Answers: Bear Power is the daily low minus the 13-day EMA. When Bear Power is negative — meaning the daily low is below the EMA — bears have momentarily pushed price below value. When Bear Power is negative but rising back toward zero in a weekly uptrend, this combination signals that bears are losing strength and the timing opportunity for a long entry is approaching.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is Bull Power in the Elder-Ray indicator and how does it time short entries?
Answers: Bull Power is the daily high minus the 13-day EMA. When Bull Power is positive and falling back toward zero in a weekly downtrend, bulls are losing control of the temporary counter-trend rally. This declining Bull Power in a bearish tide is Elder's Elder-Ray signal for a short entry timing opportunity.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does Elder handle the Second Screen when markets are in a trading range rather than a trend?
Answers: In a trading range, the weekly First Screen will not produce a clear tide direction. Elder's response is to reduce or eliminate trading entirely until a directional tide is re-established on the weekly chart. Without a clear tide, the Second Screen oscillator signals are unreliable — there is no larger force to ride, and the oscillator moves sideways.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What are the four oscillators Elder considers most suitable for the Second Screen?
Answers: The four oscillators Elder considers most suitable for the Second Screen are: (1) Force Index 2-day EMA — measures volume-weighted momentum; (2) Elder-Ray (Bull Power / Bear Power) — separates bullish from bearish intraday pressure; (3) Stochastic — identifies overbought/oversold with a dual-line confirmation mechanism; and (4) Williams %R — a close-to-range oscillator that predicts reversals through divergences.
Label: Timing
Source: BrokerXplorer – Master the Triple Screen | https://www.brokerxplorer.com/article/how-to-master-the-triple-screen-trading-strategy-1547

Questions: What does Elder say about applying the Second Screen to futures trading?
Answers: For futures trading, Elder applies the same Second Screen logic with some practical adjustments. The daily oscillator must account for the higher volatility of futures contracts, meaning overbought/oversold thresholds may need to be widened. The underlying principle — find a counter-trend wave within the dominant tide and enter when that wave exhausts — remains identical.
Label: Timing
Source: AskTraders – Elder's Triple Screening Strategy | https://www.asktraders.com/learn-to-trade/trading-guide/elders-triple-screening-strategy/

Questions: Why is finding a 'wave going against the tide' so important for timing entries?
Answers: Entering on a wave against the tide means entering after a temporary pullback or correction in the direction of the dominant trend — not at the peak of the move but at a discounted level. This improves the risk-reward ratio of the trade, tightens the distance to the logical stop-loss (the wave's extreme), and aligns the position with the tide that will carry it forward.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Alexander Elder Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/alexander-elder-triple-screen-strategy/

Questions: What does Elder say about using only the Second Screen without the First Screen?
Answers: Using the Second Screen (oscillator) alone without the context of the First Screen (tide) is one of the most common mistakes Elder identifies. An oscillator alone cannot distinguish between a genuine oversold entry opportunity and a brief pause in a strong downtrend. The First Screen's tide direction is what converts an oscillator signal from ambiguous to actionable.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder rate the quality of a Second Screen signal based on its position in the cycle?
Answers: Elder considers the highest-quality Second Screen signals to be: (1) an oscillator reaching its extreme zone and beginning to reverse in a clear trend; (2) a divergence between price and the oscillator at the wave extreme; (3) the first reversal after a long period of oscillator compression. The lowest quality signals are oscillator moves that barely reach the midpoint before reversing.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the role of volume in validating Second Screen timing signals?
Answers: Elder teaches that a daily pullback accompanied by declining volume is more bullish — the correction is happening on thin conviction — while a pullback on expanding volume suggests more serious selling that may not be easily reversed. The Force Index on the Second Screen automatically incorporates volume into its calculation, giving Elder a volume-weighted confirmation of the wave's character.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does the concept of 'riding the wave' describe Elder's entry timing?
Answers: Elder's phrase 'riding the wave' describes the timing goal precisely: entering at the moment the counter-trend wave has peaked (or troughed) and is about to reverse back in the direction of the dominant tide. The trader is not trying to predict or catch the exact top or bottom of the wave — just to position themselves as the wave exhausts and the tide takes over again.
Label: Timing
Source: FBS Analytics – Triple Screen Trading Strategy | https://fbs.com/analytics/tips/triple-screen-trading-strategy-21775

Questions: How does the oscillator's position on the day of the weekly chart review affect Second Screen timing?
Answers: Elder performs the First Screen analysis on the weekend using weekly charts. On Monday, the Second Screen daily chart may already show an oscillator that has just turned from oversold — providing an immediate timing signal. Alternatively, the oscillator may still be declining toward oversold, requiring patience. The trader monitors the daily oscillator each day until the wave exhaustion point is reached.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the Third Screen in Elder's Triple Screen System?
Answers: The Third Screen is the short-term chart — typically the 4-hour or 1-hour chart when the Second Screen uses the daily chart. It is used exclusively for timing the precise entry — identifying the exact moment to place the order after the first two screens have established the direction and the entry window. Elder calls this the 'execution screen.'
Label: Timing
Source: TradingStrategyGuides – The Triple Screen | https://tradingstrategyguides.com/alexander-elder-trading-strategy-the-triple-screen/

Questions: Does the Third Screen require any technical indicators?
Answers: Not necessarily. Elder notes that the Third Screen does not require a separate indicator in its original form. The entry mechanism is the trailing stop technique — a mechanical order placed at a predetermined price level that is automatically activated if price moves in the direction of the trade. Many practitioners do add a short-period EMA or oscillator for confirmation, but Elder's classical design uses only price-based stop orders.
Label: Timing
Source: DailyForex – Elder Triple Screen System | https://www.dailyforex.com/forex-articles/2020/09/elder-triple-screen-system/151378

Questions: What is the trailing buy-stop technique on the Third Screen?
Answers: The trailing buy-stop technique places a buy-stop order one tick above the high of the previous day's bar. This order will be triggered only if price rises above that level, confirming that the short-term ripple is moving upward in alignment with the bullish tide. If price continues to fall, the stop is not triggered and the trader has avoided entering into further weakness.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is the trailing sell-stop technique on the Third Screen?
Answers: The trailing sell-stop technique places a sell-stop order one tick below the low of the previous day's bar. This order is triggered only if price falls below that level, confirming that the short-term ripple is moving downward in alignment with the bearish tide. If price continues to bounce, the stop is not triggered and the trader avoids shorting into temporary strength.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does the trailing buy-stop auto-update each day if not triggered?
Answers: Each day that the buy-stop is not triggered, the trader lowers the stop to one tick above the new day's high (if the previous day's high was lower) to stay current with the ripple's evolving structure. If the market continues to decline without triggering the buy-stop, the order is not filled — which is a feature, not a problem, because it prevents entry into continued weakness.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is the logic behind placing the entry one tick above (or below) the previous bar's extreme?
Answers: Placing a buy-stop one tick above the previous bar's high means the entry is triggered by the market itself demonstrating upward momentum — the price breaking above a short-term resistance level. This is a momentum confirmation: the trader is not guessing that the ripple will turn; they are waiting for price to prove it by making a higher high.
Label: Timing
Source: AskTraders – Elder's Triple Screening Strategy | https://www.asktraders.com/learn-to-trade/trading-guide/elders-triple-screening-strategy/

Questions: Can a trader enter on the Third Screen without using a trailing stop order?
Answers: Yes. Elder acknowledges that the Third Screen's function — pinpointing precise entry — can be achieved with methods other than the trailing stop. Some traders use a breakout above the previous day's high on the intraday chart as a market order trigger; others use a short-period EMA crossover on the hourly chart. The critical requirement is that the entry is in the direction of the tide and wave, not the method of execution.
Label: Timing
Source: DailyForex – Elder Triple Screen System | https://www.dailyforex.com/forex-articles/2020/09/elder-triple-screen-system/151378

Questions: What happens if the Third Screen trailing stop is never triggered in a given session?
Answers: If the trailing buy-stop is never triggered — because price continues to fall below the previous day's high — the trader simply does not enter the trade. Elder teaches this as a benefit: the Third Screen acts as a final quality filter. If the market cannot produce a higher high, the ripple has not turned bullish and the entry condition has not been met.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does the Third Screen timing relate to the concept of momentum investing?
Answers: Elder explicitly notes that the trailing buy-stop technique most closely resembles momentum investing — entering only when the market produces upward price momentum by exceeding a recent resistance level. The trader is not predicting a bottom; they are waiting for the market to begin moving upward before committing capital.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What is a 'sliding order' and how does it differ from the trailing stop technique?
Answers: A sliding order (or trailing stop order, as used in some platforms) automatically moves the stop level as each new bar forms, tracking the highest high of the most recent bar. This removes the need for manual daily adjustment. In practice, the sliding and trailing stop techniques produce equivalent results; the difference is operational rather than conceptual.
Label: Timing
Source: MQL5 – Elder's Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.mql5.com/en/blogs/post/731016

Questions: How does the Third Screen prevent entry into false signals?
Answers: By requiring an actual price breakout above (for longs) or below (for shorts) the previous bar's extreme, the Third Screen prevents entry on second-screen oscillator signals that do not result in actual price movement. An oscillator can reach oversold without the price making any upward progress — the trailing stop filters out these 'dead cat' situations where the oscillator reverses without price confirming.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does Elder apply the Third Screen for intraday traders?
Answers: For day traders using a 10-minute chart as their Third Screen, the trailing buy-stop is placed above the high of the previous 10-minute bar (not the previous day's bar). The principle is identical: a breakout above the most recent bar's high in the direction of the tide confirms that the intraday ripple has turned bullish and the entry is valid.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What timeframes constitute the three screens for a day trader according to Elder?
Answers: For a day trader targeting the 10-minute chart as their primary execution timeframe: the First Screen would be the 60-minute chart (identifying the intraday tide); the Second Screen would be the 10-minute chart (identifying overbought/oversold waves); and the Third Screen would be the 2-minute chart (for trailing stop entry precision). The same factor-of-five relationship applies.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What timeframes constitute the three screens for a swing trader targeting daily charts?
Answers: For a swing trader using the daily chart as their primary timeframe: the First Screen is the weekly chart (tide identification); the Second Screen is the daily chart (oscillator wave identification); and the Third Screen is the 4-hour or 1-hour chart (trailing stop entry precision). All three screens analyse the same security on three progressively shorter timeframes.
Label: Timing
Source: AskTraders – Elder's Triple Screening Strategy | https://www.asktraders.com/learn-to-trade/trading-guide/elders-triple-screening-strategy/

Questions: What does Elder mean when he says the Third Screen is the 'execution screen'?
Answers: The Third Screen is called the execution screen because all analytical decisions have already been made by the time the trader reaches it: the First Screen established the tide direction and the Second Screen identified the entry window. The Third Screen's only job is to provide the precise moment of execution — the exact tick at which to place the order.
Label: Timing
Source: TradingStrategyGuides – The Triple Screen | https://tradingstrategyguides.com/alexander-elder-trading-strategy-the-triple-screen/

Questions: How does the EMA crossover serve as a Third Screen entry signal?
Answers: In some implementations of the Triple Screen, traders use a short-period EMA (such as a 9-period EMA) on the Third Screen chart. A long entry is triggered when price crosses above this EMA from below — in the direction of the tide and after the wave oscillator has signalled an entry window. This crossover replaces the trailing stop technique as the mechanical entry trigger.
Label: Timing
Source: MQL5 – Elder's Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.mql5.com/en/blogs/post/731016

Questions: What confirmation factors can a trader add to the Third Screen for higher confidence?
Answers: Practitioners add several confirming factors to the Third Screen: a bounce off a key support level, a false breakout reversal pattern (a kangaroo tail), a candlestick reversal pattern (hammer, engulfing), or the Impulse System changing from red or blue to green. Each additional confirmation factor improves the precision of the entry timing.
Label: Timing
Source: RoboForex Blog – Triple Screen Trading System | https://blog.roboforex.com/blog/2020/02/18/triple-screen-trading-system-how-to/

Questions: Why does Elder say that the Third Screen can sometimes be skipped with only two screens?
Answers: Elder notes that for many trades — particularly swing trades where precision entry within the day is less critical — the Third Screen's trailing stop adds only marginal benefit over simply entering on the Second Screen's oscillator signal. He acknowledges that the system works with two screens for experienced traders who have developed judgment about entry quality.
Label: Timing
Source: DailyForex – Elder Triple Screen System | https://www.dailyforex.com/forex-articles/2020/09/elder-triple-screen-system/151378

Questions: What is a 'false downside breakout' on the Third Screen and why is it a premium entry signal?
Answers: A false downside breakout occurs when price briefly pierces below a support level (triggering stops), then immediately recovers back above it — forming a kangaroo tail on the chart. When this happens after a Second Screen oversold signal in a weekly uptrend, it represents an ideal Third Screen entry: the stop-hunters have cleared the liquidity, and the bullish tide is about to reassert strongly.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Alexander Elder Triple Screen Strategy | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/alexander-elder-triple-screen-strategy/

Questions: How does Elder's Third Screen timing philosophy reflect the professional vs. amateur distinction?
Answers: Elder uses the professional/amateur contrast explicitly in this context: amateurs jump into trades impulsively the moment they see a signal on any timeframe. Professionals wait for all three screens to align, then use the Third Screen to enter only when the market is actively moving in the intended direction — paying slightly more for the entry price but vastly reducing the probability of a false start.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the Elder Impulse System?
Answers: The Elder Impulse System is a colour-coded price bar system that combines two indicators — a 13-period exponential moving average and the MACD histogram — to identify inflection points where a trend is accelerating or decelerating. Green bars signal bullish momentum (both indicators rising); red bars signal bearish momentum (both falling); blue bars signal mixed or neutral conditions.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: In which book did Elder introduce the Impulse System?
Answers: Elder introduced the Impulse System in Come Into My Trading Room (2002). It was later incorporated into The New Trading for a Living (2014) as a central timing tool alongside the Triple Screen.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: What does a green Impulse bar signal for timing?
Answers: A green bar means both the 13-period EMA slope is rising and the MACD-Histogram is rising — indicating that both the trend and the momentum are simultaneously bullish. Elder treats a series of green bars as the optimal timing environment for holding long positions. Entry into a new long position is best timed when the green bar appears after a period of blue or red bars.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: What does a red Impulse bar signal for timing?
Answers: A red bar means both the 13-period EMA slope and the MACD-Histogram are falling — indicating that both trend and momentum are simultaneously bearish. A series of red bars is the optimal timing environment for holding short positions. The appearance of the first red bar after a series of green or blue bars is a timing signal to exit longs.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: What does a blue Impulse bar signal for timing?
Answers: A blue bar means the 13-period EMA slope and the MACD-Histogram are moving in opposite directions — neither bulls nor bears are decisively in control. Blue bars signal a mixed or transitional market. Elder advises caution during blue bar periods: they are not ideal for new entries but can represent the transition point just before a green or red bar series begins.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Elder Impulse System | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/elder-impulse-system/

Questions: How does the Impulse System help a trader time entry cautiously?
Answers: The Impulse System requires both the EMA slope and the MACD-Histogram to confirm momentum in the same direction before signalling a high-quality entry. This dual confirmation prevents entries during momentary price spikes that are not supported by genuine trend momentum — ensuring the trader enters only when the market has confirmed conviction in one direction.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'enter cautiously but exit fast' in the Impulse System context?
Answers: Elder states that the Impulse System 'encourages you to enter cautiously but exit fast.' Cautious entry means waiting for both indicators to confirm green or red before entering. Fast exit means exiting the moment the first bar transitions from green or red back toward blue — not waiting for a full reversal, but acting immediately when the confirming conditions cease.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: How does the Impulse System define the optimal exit timing?
Answers: The optimal exit signal from the Impulse System is the transition from a green bar back to a blue bar (for long exits) or from a red bar back to a blue bar (for short exits). The first blue bar after a run of green bars indicates that either the EMA slope or the MACD-Histogram has stopped rising — momentum is waning — and the position should be exited promptly.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Elder Impulse System | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/elder-impulse-system/

Questions: How does the Impulse System differ from the Triple Screen in its timing function?
Answers: The Triple Screen is a complete trade selection and timing system covering three timeframes. The Impulse System is a single-timeframe momentum-confirmation tool. Elder uses the Impulse System as a filter within the Triple Screen: before executing the Third Screen entry, he checks whether the daily chart's Impulse System is green (for longs) or red (for shorts), using it as a final confirmation gate.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: What is the 65-day EMA's role in the Impulse System?
Answers: The 65-day EMA (five times the 13-day EMA) serves as a proxy for the weekly trend on the daily chart. Elder uses the 65-day EMA to determine the longer-term tide without switching to the weekly chart. If price is above the 65-day EMA and the MACD is positive, the long-term trend is considered bullish and daily Impulse System buy signals are valid.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: How does the Impulse System prevent bad timing in ambiguous markets?
Answers: When markets are choppy or directionless, the Impulse System produces predominantly blue bars — neither green nor red in consecutive runs. This automatically discourages trading by withholding the clear colour signals needed for entry, keeping the trader in cash during unrewarding market conditions.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: How does Elder use the Impulse System to time additions to a winning position?
Answers: When a long position is open and profitable, Elder uses each new green bar as a potential timing signal to add to the position — as long as the First Screen tide remains bullish and the 2% Rule permits the additional risk. The green bar confirms that the bullish momentum is continuing and that adding to the position is not chasing a weakening trend.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: What is the Impulse System's indicator combination and how are the colours generated?
Answers: The Impulse System uses a 13-period EMA and the MACD Histogram with standard settings (12, 26, 9). Green bars are generated when both the 13-period EMA slope is positive (current EMA > previous EMA) AND the MACD Histogram is rising (current bar > previous bar). Red bars require both to be declining. Blue bars occur when they are in disagreement.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: How can a trader apply the Impulse System across different timeframes?
Answers: The Impulse System is timeframe-agnostic. Traders using weekly charts for their intermediate timeframe move to monthly charts for the long-term Impulse confirmation. Day traders applying the system to 10-minute charts check the 60-minute Impulse for trend context. The rule is always to trade the Impulse System signals on the intermediate timeframe only when they align with the long-term trend.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: What does Elder say about the risk of ignoring Impulse System red bars on open positions?
Answers: Elder is categorical: when a position is long and the Impulse System transitions to red, exit the position. Holding through a red bar means holding through a period when both trend and momentum are working against you. The risk-reward of continuing to hold deteriorates rapidly from the first red bar — the position should be exited at the next available price.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Elder Impulse System | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/elder-impulse-system/

Questions: What is the MACD Histogram and who developed it?
Answers: The MACD Histogram measures the distance between the MACD line (the difference between two EMAs) and its signal line (a 9-day EMA of the MACD line). It was developed by Thomas Aspray in 1986 and became central to Elder's trading system. Elder considers it superior to the raw MACD lines for timing because it shows not just who controls the market but the strength of that control and how it is changing.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: How does Elder use the MACD Histogram slope for timing on the weekly First Screen?
Answers: On the weekly chart, the slope of each successive MACD Histogram bar — whether the current bar is higher or lower than the previous one — determines the tide direction. A rising bar means bullish tide regardless of the histogram's position above or below zero; a falling bar means bearish tide. The slope change from one bar to the next is the timing signal that switches the allowed trading direction.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'most common' MACD Histogram signal according to Elder?
Answers: The most common MACD Histogram signal, according to Elder, is the slope signal: when the current histogram bar is higher than the previous bar, bulls are in control; when lower, bears are in control. This signal occurs on every price bar, making it the routine timing indicator for daily market analysis in Elder's framework.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: What is the 'rare and extremely strong' MACD Histogram signal Elder identifies?
Answers: The rare, extremely strong signal is the divergence between the MACD Histogram and price: when price makes a new extreme (new high or new low) but the histogram does not confirm with its own new extreme, creating a divergence. Elder states this signal occurs only a few times per year in any market, but when it appears it represents one of the most powerful timing signals available.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: What is a bearish divergence on the MACD Histogram and how does it affect timing?
Answers: A bearish divergence occurs when price makes a new high but the MACD Histogram makes a lower peak than at the previous high. This signals that bullish momentum is fading even as price extends upward — a warning that the uptrend may be near exhaustion. Elder uses this divergence to time exits from long positions and to begin looking for short-entry opportunities.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is a bullish divergence on the MACD Histogram and how does it affect timing?
Answers: A bullish divergence occurs when price makes a new low but the MACD Histogram makes a shallower trough than at the previous low. This signals that bearish momentum is weakening even as price continues to fall — indicating that the downtrend may be near its end. Elder uses this divergence to time exits from short positions and to begin looking for long-entry opportunities.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder consider the MACD Histogram superior to the MACD lines for timing?
Answers: The MACD lines (the fast and slow EMAs) are lagging indicators that confirm trends after they are established. The MACD Histogram is more sensitive because it shows the acceleration or deceleration of momentum on a bar-by-bar basis — catching trend changes earlier than the underlying MACD lines. For timing, earlier signals — even at the cost of slightly more false signals — are generally more valuable.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: How does the zero-line crossing of the MACD Histogram affect timing?
Answers: When the MACD Histogram crosses from below zero to above it, bulls have taken control of momentum. When it crosses from above to below zero, bears have taken control. Elder uses zero-line crossings as timing signals for longer-term traders who are not trying to catch every short-term oscillation. Crossings confirm major tide changes; slope reversals catch earlier, more nuanced timing opportunities.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does it mean when the MACD Histogram makes successively larger bars in one direction?
Answers: Successively larger bars in one direction — each bar taller than the previous — signal accelerating momentum. In an uptrend, growing histogram bars confirm that bulls are gaining strength and the trend is developing momentum. This is the timing signal for holding long positions confidently and potentially adding to them rather than looking for exit opportunities.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: What does it mean when the MACD Histogram bars begin shrinking?
Answers: Shrinking histogram bars — each bar shorter than the previous — signal decelerating momentum, even if the bars are still positive (for an uptrend) or negative (for a downtrend). Elder treats bar shrinkage as an early warning of potential reversal — not yet an exit signal, but a cue to tighten stops and begin monitoring other indicators for confirmation of the trend's exhaustion.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system

Questions: How does Elder prefer to set MACD parameters for timing purposes?
Answers: Elder typically uses the standard MACD settings of 12, 26, and 9. For trend-following purposes, some practitioners suggest setting the first EMA at half the length of the dominant cycle and the second at half the length of the first. Elder acknowledges that parameter optimisation can improve performance in specific markets but cautions against over-optimising to historical data.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: Why does Elder prefer using the MACD Histogram on weekly charts over shorter timeframes?
Answers: On shorter timeframes (hourly or daily), the MACD Histogram ticks up and down frequently, making it impractical to trade every slope change. On the weekly chart, slope changes are more meaningful because each bar represents five days of consolidated market activity. The weekly MACD Histogram's slope therefore reflects genuine tide shifts rather than intraday noise.
Label: Timing
Source: ElearnMarkets – MACD Histogram Demystified | https://blog.elearnmarkets.com/macd-histogram-demystified/

Questions: What combination of indicators does Elder use to create the Impulse System from the MACD?
Answers: The Impulse System takes the 13-period EMA (for trend direction) and the MACD-Histogram (for momentum direction) and combines them into a simple binary rule: when both are rising together, the bar is green; when both are falling, the bar is red; otherwise, the bar is blue. This simplification of the MACD into a colour code makes timing decisions faster and more objective.
Label: Timing
Source: QuantifiedStrategies – Elder Impulse System | https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/elder-impulse-system/

Questions: How does Elder use the MACD Histogram to measure the 'power of bulls and bears'?
Answers: Elder teaches that the MACD Histogram reflects the balance of power between bulls and bears. When the histogram is above zero and rising, bulls have greater power than bears and are gaining strength. When below zero and falling, bears have the advantage and are growing stronger. The magnitude of the histogram bars reflects the intensity of the controlling group's dominance.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about trading the MACD Histogram in isolation?
Answers: Elder explicitly cautions against using the MACD Histogram as a standalone timing tool. Like all indicators, it produces false signals in certain market environments — particularly in choppy, non-trending conditions where it oscillates without directional conviction. The MACD Histogram is most powerful when used within the Triple Screen framework, where the First Screen's tide context filters out the majority of false daily signals.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What are the two primary conditions that trigger an exit in Elder's timing framework?
Answers: Elder identifies two primary exit conditions: (1) the weekly MACD Histogram (First Screen tide) reverses direction — the tide has changed; and (2) a divergence between the daily oscillator and price on the Second Screen indicates that momentum is failing. Either condition on its own is sufficient to begin the exit process; both together confirm a high-urgency exit.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: When does Elder use the weekly chart to time an exit?
Answers: Elder uses the weekly chart to time the exit when the weekly MACD Histogram reverses slope — signalling that the dominant tide has changed direction. This is the highest-timeframe exit signal, indicating that the market's fundamental bias has shifted and that holding the position further means holding against the new tide.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does a bearish divergence on the daily chart time an exit from a long position?
Answers: When the daily chart shows price making a new high but the MACD Histogram or oscillator makes a lower peak (bearish divergence), Elder treats this as an urgent timing signal to exit the long position. The divergence indicates that upward momentum is failing — bulls are making a new high but with declining strength — and that the trend is vulnerable to reversal.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: How does Elder use the oscillator to time profit-taking on a long trade?
Answers: When the daily oscillator on the Second Screen reaches overbought territory — Stochastic above 80, Williams %R above -20, Force Index at an extreme positive level — Elder treats this as a timing signal that the intermediate wave has reached its peak. At this point he begins tightening the trailing stop or takes partial profits, protecting the accumulated gain.
Label: Timing
Source: ForexFactory – Third Screen Strategy (Elder) | https://www.forexfactory.com/thread/382032-third-screen-strategy-dr-alexander-elder

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'the only time you have complete objectivity is before you enter'?
Answers: Before entering a trade, the trader has no financial stake and can evaluate the exit plan clearly and rationally. Once in a position, fear of loss and hope of profit distort judgment. Elder insists that exit levels — both stops and targets — be defined before entry, because the pre-entry plan is the only objective exit timing decision the trader will ever make for that trade.
Label: Timing
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the Impulse System colour change time exits from long positions?
Answers: The Impulse System signals a long exit when the price bars transition from green to blue or red. The first non-green bar after a green run signals that bullish momentum is no longer confirmed by both the EMA and MACD-Histogram simultaneously. Elder exits long positions on the close of the first bar that is no longer green — without waiting for a full red bar.
Label: Timing
Source: StockCharts ChartSchool – Elder Impulse System | https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-types/elder-impulse-system
